comments.comment: It is assumed that ionic cyanide is not released from coal despite the estimation (USEPA AP42 emission factor) which indicates that there is.  The justification for this is the emission factor is based on sampling which did not detect the pollutant in any of the sampling runs used to develop the factor.  Thus, the emission factor used represents a factor based on one-half the of the detection limit.  Furthermore, due to the environment in which any free cyanide would be released (high temperature/high SO2 concentration coal combustion flue gas), it is assumed that any free cyanide would be incinerated or oxidized (and released as a complex).

comments.comment:
As noted within the comments, the calculation of Hydrochloric Acid was updated to incorporate a more up to date emission factor.  Prior to 2012 the emission factor used was 0.007 kg/tonne of coal and it was based on stack testing done on January 13, 1999. Since this data is over 10 years old it is deemed as noncurrent.  Therefore, we have now applied the emission factor from USEPA AP 42, Chapter 1.1, Table 1.1-15 of 1.2 (Ib/ton) (which converts to 0.6 kg/tonne of coal) to our calculation.

We have noticed that we mistakenly switched the Hydrogen Fluoride emission factor with the Hydrochloric Acid emission factor within our calculation spreadsheets.  We will update the online submission.


Updated emission factor  to source from USEPA AP 42, Chapter 1.1

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment:
Hydrogen Fluoride

As noted within the comments, the calculation of Hydrogen Fluoride was updated to incorporate a more up to date emission factor.  Prior to 2012 the emission factor used was 0.007 kg/tonne of coal and it was based on stack testing done on January 13, 1999. Since this data is over 10 years old it is deemed as noncurrent.  Therefore, we have now applied the emission factor from USEPA AP 42, Chapter 1.1, Table 1.1-15 of 0.15 (Ib/ton) (which converts to 0.075 kg/tonne of coal) to our calculation.

We have noticed that we mistakenly switched the Hydrogen Fluoride emission factor with the Hydrochloric Acid emission factor within our calculation spreadsheets.  We will update the online submission.


Updated emission factor  to source from USEPA AP 42, Chapter 1.1

comments.comment: It is assumed that ionic cyanide is not released from coal despite the estimation (USEPA AP42 emission factor) which indicates that there is.  The justification for this is the emission factor is based on sampling which did not detect the pollutant in any of the sampling runs used to develop the factor.  Thus, the emission factor used represents a factor based on one-half the of the detection limit.  Furthermore, due to the environment in which any free cyanide would be released (high temperature/high SO2 concentration coal combustion flue gas), it is assumed that any free cyanide would be incinerated or oxidized (and released as a complex).

Higher operational hours in 2019 compared to 2018 led to higher air emissions. These higher air emissions combined with water emissions in the blowdown water lead to total emissions being over the NPRI threshold for cobalt reporting.
Speciated VOCs (part 5 substances) were previously below the 1 tonne reporting threshold. Due to higher rates of gas burn, VOCs now meet the NPRI reporting threshold.

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment: It is assumed that ionic cyanide is not released from coal despite the estimation (USEPA AP42 emission factor) which indicates that there is.  The justification for this is the emission factor is based on sampling which did not detect the pollutant in any of the sampling runs used to develop the factor.  Thus, the emission factor used represents a factor based on one-half the of the detection limit.  Furthermore, due to the environment in which any free cyanide would be released (high temperature/high SO2 concentration coal combustion flue gas), it is assumed that any free cyanide would be incinerated or oxidized (and released as a complex).

comments.comment: It is assumed that ionic cyanide is not released from coal despite the estimation (USEPA AP42 emission factor) which indicates that there is.  The justification for this is the emission factor is based on sampling which did not detect the pollutant in any of the sampling runs used to develop the factor.  Thus, the emission factor used represents a factor based on one-half the of the detection limit.  Furthermore, due to the environment in which any free cyanide would be released (high temperature/high SO2 concentration coal combustion flue gas), it is assumed that any free cyanide would be incinerated or oxidized (and released as a complex).
